title: National Culture and AI Governance - The Cultural Origins of Differences in AI Regulation
text:
## National Culture and AI Governance: The Cultural Origins of Differences in AI Regulation

Stefan Pasch 1

## Abstract

Despite  widespread  agreement  on  the  importance  of  responsible  artificial  intelligence  (AI), countries differ markedly in how strongly AI is governed, ranging from no government action to non-binding guidance and fully enforceable legal regulation. Yet the sources of these differences remain poorly understood. This study examines whether and how national cultural value systems help explain cross-national variation in the institutional strength of AI governance frameworks. Using  cross-national  data  from  the  Global  Index  on  Responsible  AI  (GIRAI),  we  measure  AI governance  across  nine  core  governance  areas,  including  transparency,  accountability,  human oversight,  and  safety.  We  capture  governance  strength  as  the  degree  of  legal  and  institutional consolidation of these measures, distinguishing between non-binding instruments (such as strategies and  ethical  guidelines)  and  enforceable  regulatory  obligations.  We  then  relate  this  measure  to national  cultural  value  systems  using  Hofstede's  cultural  dimensions.  Based  on  cross-sectional regression analyses covering 50 countries, we find that individualism and uncertainty avoidance are positively associated with more institutionalized and enforceable AI governance, while indulgence is negatively related. Importantly, when distinguishing between different levels of AI governance strength, we find that national culture is associated only with enforceable legal regulation, whereas softer forms of AI governance, such as non-binding guidelines, show no systematic relationship with cultural values. The findings highlight the cultural foundations of enforceable AI governance and help explain why countries differ in their willingness to translate responsible AI principles into binding institutional commitments.

Keywords : AI governance, AI regulation, Responsible AI, National culture, Hofstede's cultural dimensions

## 1. Introduction

As artificial intelligence becomes increasingly embedded in corporate decision-making, public administration,  and  everyday  digital  services,  it  has  emerged  as  a  central  object  of  public policy.  Governments  now  confront  a  growing  set  of  challenges  related  to  algorithmic accountability,  fairness,  transparency,  and  safety,  particularly  as  automated  systems  shape consequential decisions affecting individuals and societies (Mittelstadt, et al., 2016; Floridi et al., 2018; Pasch & Cha, 2025). In response, national authorities and international organizations have accelerated efforts to develop approaches to governing AI that aim to align technological deployment with ethical values and safety (Daly et al., 2019; Cole, 2024).

Despite  the  shared  technological  foundations  of  AI,  national  regulatory  responses  vary substantially.  Some  countries  have  adopted  comparatively  strong  and  institutionalized  AI governance  approaches,  characterized  by  binding  rules,  formal  oversight  mechanisms,  and enforceable  procedural  safeguards.  For  example,  Germany  has  embedded  AI  governance within  binding  national  legal  and  administrative  frameworks,  resulting  in  comparatively

1 Division of Social Science & AI, Hankuk University of Foreign Studies: stefan.pasch@outlook.com

formalized and enforceable oversight (Tuzov & Lin, 2024). Similarly, Canada has adopted enforceable public-law mechanisms for governing AI use, particularly in the public sector, emphasizing transparency, accountability, and avenues for redress (Government of Canada, 2025). By contrast, other countries have emphasized non-binding strategies, ethical guidelines, or  voluntary principles, placing greater weight on flexibility, innovation, and industry selfregulation.  For  example,  Japan's  AI  governance  approach  relies  largely  on  non-mandatory guidelines and industry-oriented principles (METI, 2019; Habuka, 2023), while Singapore has similarly promoted voluntary governance instruments such as model frameworks and ethical toolkits rather than binding regulation (PDPC, 2020; Allen et al., 2025).

This cross-national heterogeneity - ranging from countries with little or no AI governance initiatives,  through  predominantly  soft-law  approaches,  to  legally  enforceable  regulatory frameworks - raises an important question: why do some countries translate responsible AI principles  into  binding  institutional  commitments,  while  others  refrain  from  doing  so? Understanding the sources of these differences in the institutional depth and legal maturity of AI governance remains a central, yet underexplored, challenge.

Existing scholarship has largely approached AI governance from normative, ethical, or policydesign perspectives, focusing on principles, best practices, and regulatory architectures (e.g., Batool et al., 2025; Veale & Borgesius, 2021). A growing empirical literature examines public perceptions of AI and support for regulation across countries, showing that attitudes toward AI risks and oversight vary systematically across cultural contexts (Shin et al., 2022; Wilczek et al., 2025). At the same time, comparative political economy research emphasizes that longstanding cultural value systems shape regulatory preferences, risk tolerance, and expectations of state intervention (Hofstede, 2001; Licht et al., 2007). Yet, these strands of research have rarely been brought together. To date, it remains unclear whether and how national cultural value  systems  help  explain  cross-national  variation  in  the  institutional  strength  of  AI governance frameworks.

This paper addresses this gap by examining the relationship between national cultural value systems and responsible AI governance at the country level. We argue that cultural orientations related  to  individual  autonomy,  uncertainty  tolerance,  long-term  orientation,  and  normative restraint  are  associated  with  differences  in  how  societies  approach  risk  management, accountability,  and  the  role  of  law  in  governing  emerging  technologies,  and  that  these differences are reflected in the degree to which AI governance frameworks are institutionalized and legally enforceable.

Empirically,  we  combine  cultural  indicators  from  Hofstede's  widely  used  national  culture dimensions with newly available cross-national data from the Global Index on Responsible AI (GIRAI)  (Adams  et  al.,  2024).  GIRAI  provides  systematically  coded  information  on responsible AI governance efforts across nine core areas, including transparency, accountability, human oversight, and safety. Rather than focusing solely on the presence of policy activity, we operationalize AI governance strength as the degree of institutionalization across these dimensions, distinguishing between non-binding initiatives, formal plans or draft legislation aimed at introducing enforceable regulation, and fully enacted and enforceable laws.

This approach allows us to capture meaningful variation in the legal and institutional maturity of national AI governance frameworks.

Using cross-sectional regression analyses covering 50 countries for which comparable cultural and governance data are available, we examine how different cultural dimensions relate to overall AI governance strength and to specific forms of institutionalization. Our results indicate that national cultural value systems are systematically associated with AI governance strength. Individualism and uncertainty avoidance are positively associated with stronger AI governance,  while  indulgence  is  negatively  related  to  AI  governance  strength.  Long-term orientation  exhibits  a  positive  but  less  robust  relationship,  whereas  power  distance  and masculinity  show  no  consistent  associations.  Importantly,  these  associations  are  driven  by cross-national differences in fully enacted and enforceable AI regulation, whereas variation in non-binding guidelines and preparatory policy initiatives shows no systematic relationship with cultural values.

This study makes three contributions to the emerging literature on AI governance. First, it introduces  national  culture  as  an  explanatory  factor  for  cross-national  variation  in  AI governance  outcomes,  drawing  attention  to  how  differences  in  societal  value  systems  are reflected  in  the  strength  of  AI  governance  frameworks.  Second,  it  advances  comparative institutional and cultural theory by demonstrating how deeply embedded value systems shape regulatory responses to complex and uncertain technologies. Third, by distinguishing between non-binding initiatives  and  enforceable  regulation,  the  study  shows  that  cultural  influences matter most when governments commit to legally constraining AI-related risks. Together, these findings  highlight  that  global  variation  in  AI  governance  reflects  not  only  differences  in capacity or policy diffusion, but also enduring societal preferences regarding risk, authority, and institutional commitment.

## 2. Theoretical Background

## 2.1. National Culture and Technology Governance

National culture has long been recognized as a fundamental driver of how societies organize authority,  perceive  risk,  and  construct  governance  systems.  Research  in  cross-cultural psychology, sociology, and comparative political economy shows that national culture shapes institutional preferences, regulatory behavior, and long-term governance trajectories. Hofstede's  (1984,  2001)  framework  remains  one  of  the  most  frequently  used  measures  of cultural  values,  identifying  dimensions  such  as  power  distance,  individualism,  uncertainty avoidance, and long-term orientation as key determinants of how societies structure authority, manage  uncertainty,  and  distribute  responsibility.  Complementary  approaches  -  including Schwartz's value theory (1992, 2006) and the GLOBE study (House et al., 2004) - likewise emphasize  persistent  societal  norms  that  guide  collective  decision-making  and  governance styles.

These  cultural  orientations  influence  not  only  interpersonal  behavior  but  also  broader institutional tendencies, such as legal formalism, state interventionism, and public expectations of regulatory protection (Kelley & Evans, 1995; Licht et al., 2007). Comparative institutional research  also  highlights  the  path-dependent  nature  of  governance  systems,  showing  how cultural values interact with historical and legal traditions to shape regulatory regimes over time  (La  Porta  et  al.,  1999;  North,  1990).  Together,  this  literature  establishes  culture  as  a meaningful determinant of governance patterns and regulatory choices.

Building on these foundations, a growing body of scholarship examines how cultural factors shape public policy responses to technological change. Studies on data protection, cybersecurity,  biotechnology,  and  digital  governance  consistently  find  that  cultural  values influence societies' willingness to embrace formal regulation, perceptions of appropriate state oversight, and tendencies toward precautionary versus innovation-oriented policies (Onumo et al., 2017; Milberg et al., 1995). For example, uncertainty avoidance has been linked to stronger risk  mitigation  rules  (Milberg  et  al.,  1995),  while  individualism  correlates  with  greater emphasis on rights-based protections and cybersecurity (Onumo et al., 2017). This literature shows that governance of emerging technologies is deeply embedded in social and cultural contexts, suggesting that AI regulation - an area characterized by high uncertainty and ethical complexity - may also be shaped by culturally grounded regulatory expectations.

Direct empirical evidence on cultural influences in AI emerges from recent studies examining public  perceptions  of  artificial  intelligence.  An  expanding  line  of  research  demonstrates substantial  cross-cultural  variation  in  how  individuals  interpret  the  risks,  benefits,  and appropriate oversight mechanisms for AI systems. For example, Wilczek et al. (2025) show that  uncertainty  avoidance  strongly  predicts  public  support  for  government  regulation  over industry  self-regulation  in  Europe,  mediated  by  perceived  AI  risks.  Brauner  et  al.  (2024) compare expectations and risk perceptions in Germany and China and find significant cultural differences in trust, perceived value, and trade-offs associated with AI deployment. Similarly, Shin  et  al.  (2022)  demonstrate  that  cultural  dimensions  explain  how  individuals  evaluate algorithmic news systems, influencing perceptions of fairness, accuracy, and accountability. Across these studies, cultural factors such as uncertainty avoidance, individualism, and power distance consistently shape risk attitudes and preferences for institutional oversight. Although these findings focus on public perceptions rather than national institutions, they suggest that cultural  orientations  may  influence  broader  societal  demand  for  -  or  resistance  to  -  AI regulation.

A parallel line of research examines how AI systems themselves encode or reproduce cultural value structures. Kharchenko et al. (2024) demonstrate that large language models (LLMs) express culturally patterned responses aligned with Hofstede's dimensions, indicating that AI outputs may implicitly reflect societal value systems. Other studies show that LLMs exhibit culturally specific moral judgments, communication styles, or normative assumptions, raising concerns about cross-cultural bias and representation in AI systems (Sakib & Das, 2024; Tao et al., 2024). While this literature focuses on cultural patterns within AI models rather than on national governance structures, it reinforces the broader insight that the interaction between AI systems and cultural contexts is a recurring theme across different domains of AI research.

Despite growing interest in the cultural dimensions of AI, research has primarily examined individual-level perceptions, cross-cultural user attitudes, or cultural biases in AI systems. To date,  relatively  little  research  has  systematically  examined  whether  and  how  cultural  value systems shape national approaches to AI governance, particularly the legal and institutional structures through which governments regulate and oversee AI technologies. Existing work offers important insights into how people perceive AI or how AI reflects cultural norms, but it does not address whether societies with different cultural profiles build more comprehensive, enforceable,  or  mature  AI  governance  regimes.  Given  the  rapid  global  expansion  of  AI regulation  and  the  diversity  of  national  responses,  this  constitutes  a  significant  gap  in  the literature.

Addressing this gap requires a framework that captures stable, cross-national differences in societal  values  and  allows  for  systematic  comparison  across  countries.  Hofstede's  cultural dimensions  provide  such  a  framework  and  have  been  widely  used  to  explain  variation  in regulatory preferences, institutional design, and governance outcomes across national contexts.

## 2.2. Hofstede's Cultural Dimensions as Predictors of Governance

Hofstede's  cultural  dimensions  constitute  one  of  the  most  widely  used  and  empirically grounded frameworks for comparing national value systems. Originally developed using largescale cross-national survey data, Hofstede's framework has been refined and updated over time to incorporate additional countries and more recent, nationally representative datasets curated by Hofstede Insights (Hofstede, 2001; Minkov & Hofstede, 2012). The framework identifies stable, deeply embedded cultural orientations that systematically differ across societies, and it has become a cornerstone in cross-cultural research due to its conceptual clarity, extensive country  coverage,  and  strong  empirical  track  record  across  disciplines  (Taras,  Kirkman,  & Steel, 2010; Beugelsdijk, Kostova, & Roth, 2017).

The six dimensions - power distance, individualism, masculinity, uncertainty avoidance, longterm orientation, and indulgence - capture societal preferences related to authority, autonomy, risk management, time horizons, and normative restraint. These dimensions have been linked to a wide range of political, economic, organizational, and technological outcomes, including governance quality, regulatory styles, innovation processes, risk perceptions, and institutional trust (e.g., Licht et al., 2007; Taras et al., 2010). Because expectations about accountability, transparency, state responsibility, and precaution are culturally shaped, Hofstede's measures offer a valuable lens for analyzing cross-national variation in AI governance. Moreover, the framework provides comparable and stable indicators for a large set of countries included in the GIRAI dataset, making it well-suited for examining institutional differences in responsible AI governance.

## 2.3 Cultural Dimensions and Expected Effects on AI Governance

Based on the broader literature linking cultural value systems to regulatory preferences and institutional arrangements, we now develop expectations for how each of Hofstede's cultural dimensions  may  shape  the  strength  of  responsible  AI  governance  initiatives.  For  each dimension, we outline the underlying cultural logic and derive a corresponding hypothesis. Figure  1  provides  a  conceptual  overview  of  the  proposed  relationships  between  cultural dimensions and AI governance strength.

## 2.3.1. Power Distance (PDI)

Power distance captures the extent to which inequality and hierarchical authority are accepted as  legitimate  in  a  society  (Hofstede,  2001).  High-PDI  contexts  tend  to  centralize  decisionmaking  and  tolerate  asymmetric  power  relations,  whereas  low-PDI  contexts  emphasize accountability, participation, and more horizontal forms of authority. Prior research links lower power distance to stronger rule of law, greater constraints on elites, and more robust checks and balances (Licht et al., 2007; Treisman, 2000). Conversely, high power distance is often associated with weaker accountability mechanisms and lower pressure for transparent, rightsbased regulation.

Responsible AI governance, especially in areas such as transparency, redress, and accountability, typically requires institutionalized checks on discretionary power  and mechanisms that enable individuals to contest AI-related harms. Societies with lower power distance may therefore be more likely to adopt comprehensive and enforceable AI governance frameworks that constrain both state and corporate actors. In contrast, high-PDI societies may either rely on opaque, top-down approaches or underprioritize citizen-facing safeguards.

## Hypothesis 1 (H1): Power distance is negatively associated with AI governance strength.

## 2.3.2 Individualism (IDV)

Individualism reflects the extent to which societies emphasize individual autonomy, personal rights, and self-expression over collective obligations and group conformity (Hofstede, 2001; Triandis, 2018). High-IDV cultures tend to prioritize civil liberties, procedural fairness, and legal  protection  of  individuals,  while  collectivist  societies  more  strongly  emphasize  group harmony, traditions, and religion (Bierbrauer, 1994). Empirical work links individualism to stronger rights-based institutions, higher demand for privacy protections, and greater emphasis on due process (Milberg et al., 1995; Onumo et al., 2017).

Responsible AI governance is closely tied to the protection of individuals from algorithmic harm, including safeguards such as transparency, explainability, contestability, and access to remedy. In more individualistic societies, institutional and legal frameworks are more likely to embed such rights and to prioritize enforceable regulatory mechanisms protecting users and citizens against AI-related risks.

## Hypothesis 2 (H2): Individualism is positively associated with AI governance strength.

## 2.3.3 Masculinity (MAS)

Masculinity  describes  the  extent  to  which  a  society  values  competition,  achievement,  and performance  (high  MAS)  versus  cooperation,  care,  and  quality  of  life  (low  MAS  or  high 'femininity') (Hofstede, 2001). High-MAS cultures emphasize economic competitiveness and assertiveness,  whereas  more  feminine  cultures  prioritize  social  welfare,  inclusiveness,  and protection of the vulnerable. Prior studies suggest that more feminine societies are more willing to regulate in favor of social and environmental protection, whereas masculine societies tend to give greater weight to economic performance and competitiveness (Hofstede, 2001; Husted, 2005).

Responsible AI governance often implies constraining certain uses of AI, imposing compliance costs, or slowing deployment in order to protect fundamental rights and societal well-being. Societies  that  are  more  care-  and  welfare-oriented  may  therefore  be  more  inclined  to institutionalize strong AI governance frameworks, while highly masculine contexts may be more reluctant to adopt stringent rules that could be perceived as limiting competitiveness and innovation.

## Hypothesis 3 (H3): Masculinity is negatively associated with AI governance strength.

## 2.3.4 Uncertainty Avoidance (UAI)

Uncertainty avoidance captures the degree to which societies feel threatened by ambiguity and unstructured  situations,  and  thus  prefer  clear  rules,  formal  procedures,  and  predictability (Hofstede,  2001).  High-UAI  cultures  tend  to  respond  to  uncertainty  by  creating  detailed regulations  and  formalized  safeguards,  whereas  low-UAI  cultures  are  more  tolerant  of ambiguity and flexible arrangements. Cross-cultural research links uncertainty avoidance to stronger preferences for risk mitigation, legal formalism, and structured regulatory responses (Hofstede, 2001; Hofstede et al., 2010). Recent work also shows that individuals in high-UAI societies  are  more  likely  to  perceive  AI  as  risky  and  demand  government  regulation  over industry self-regulation (Wilczek et al., 2025).

Because AI is characterized by significant uncertainty regarding its societal, ethical, and safety impacts,  high-UAI  countries  can  be  expected  to  respond  with  more  comprehensive  and enforceable  governance  frameworks.  They  are  more  likely  to  adopt  binding  rules,  impact assessments, and formal oversight mechanisms in order to reduce perceived uncertainty and risk.

## Hypothesis  4  (H4):  Uncertainty  avoidance  is  positively  associated  with  AI  governance strength.

## 2.3.5 Long-Term Orientation (LTO)

Long-term orientation reflects the extent to which societies prioritize future-oriented planning, perseverance,  and  deferred  gratification  over  short-term  concerns  and  immediate  returns

(Hofstede, 2001; Minkov & Hofstede, 2012). High-LTO societies are more likely to invest in long-term institutional capacity, education, and innovation systems, and to adopt policies that address slow-moving or uncertain risks (Durach & Wiengarten, 2017; Holý & Evan, 2022).

AI governance involves anticipating and managing complex, long-horizon societal and ethical implications,  often  before  harms  are  fully  observable.  Countries  with  a  stronger  long-term orientation may be more willing to invest in robust legal frameworks, oversight institutions, and procedural safeguards to ensure sustainable and responsible AI deployment. This suggests a positive link between LTO and the degree to which AI governance is institutionalized and legally enforceable.

## Hypothesis 5 (H5): Long-term orientation is positively associated with AI governance strength.

## 2.3.6 Indulgence (IND)

Indulgence refers to the extent to which societies allow relatively free gratification of desires and  emphasize  enjoyment,  leisure,  and  personal  freedom,  as  opposed  to  restraint-oriented societies  that  value  self-control,  normative  discipline,  and  tight  regulation  of  behavior (Hofstede, 2001). Research suggests that restrained societies tend to maintain stricter social norms,  higher behavioral regulation, and a stronger acceptance of authority-imposed restrictions (Minkov, 2018; Minkov & Hofstede, 2012). These traits map closely onto contexts in which formal regulation and state-led oversight are perceived as legitimate and necessary tools for maintaining order.

Responsible  AI  governance,  particularly  when  institutionalized  through  legally  binding measures,  often  requires  restrictions  on  organizational  discretion,  mandatory  compliance processes, and enforcement mechanisms to mitigate societal risks. Societies characterized by restraint may therefore be more willing to accept strong regulatory interventions and limitations on AI deployment in the interest of preserving social order and preventing misuse. In contrast, highly  indulgent  societies  -  with  their  emphasis  on  personal  freedom,  permissiveness,  and weaker  normative  constraints  -  may  be  less  inclined  to  support  extensive  regulatory frameworks  that  impose  restrictions  on  technological  actors  or  constrain  innovation  for precautionary reasons.

Hypothesis 6 (H6): Indulgence is negatively associated with AI governance strength.

Figure 1. Conceptual framework summarizing hypothesized associations between national cultural dimensions and AI governance strength.

## 3. Methodology

## 3.1 Measuring AI Governance

Comparing responsible AI governance across countries requires indicators that capture not only whether governments engage with AI policy, but also the extent to which regulatory measures are formally institutionalized. To operationalize these differences, this study draws on raw data from  the  Global  Index  on  Responsible  AI  (GIRAI)  (Adams  et  al.,  2024),  a  comparative assessment developed in partnership with international organizations such as the Government of  Canada,  IDRC,  AI4D  Africa,  and  USAID.  The  2024  edition  provides  standardized evaluations for more than 130 countries, based on expert reviews and verification of publicly accessible policy documents produced during 2023.

GIRAI  offers  detailed  information  on  the  institutional  and  procedural  components  of responsible AI governance. We rely on the index's Responsible AI Governance section, which tracks  government  activity  across  nine  core  areas  that  together  reflect  the  institutional foundations of responsible AI:

- Access to remedy and redress: mechanisms enabling individuals to challenge, appeal, or seek compensation for AI-related harms.
- Human oversight and determination: requirements ensuring meaningful human involvement in automated or AI-supported decision-making.
- Impact assessments: procedures for evaluating potential societal, ethical, or legal risks associated with AI systems.
- National AI policy: overarching governmental strategies that coordinate AI development and governance objectives.
- Proportionality and do-no-harm: principles aimed at balancing innovation with the prevention or mitigation of adverse impacts.
- Public procurement: standards governing the responsible use of AI in government purchasing and public-sector deployment.
- Responsibility and accountability: rules that allocate oversight duties, responsibilities, or liability for AI outcomes.
- Safety, accuracy, and reliability: requirements addressing the technical robustness and dependable performance of AI systems.
- Transparency and explainability: obligations to disclose information about AI use and to enable understanding of algorithmic decisions.

For  each  country-dimension  pair,  national  experts  coded  whether  relevant  government measures exist and verified the evidence through publicly accessible legal texts, draft bills, strategies, or regulatory documents. Measures were only recorded when they could be linked to  an  identifiable  official  source,  ensuring  that  the  dataset  reflects  verifiable,  officially documented government instruments.

Importantly,  GIRAI  does  not  merely  record  whether  a  policy  exists  in  a  given  area  of responsible AI governance, but explicitly distinguishes between  different degrees of institutionalization and legal commitment. Countries often differ not in whether they address a governance issue, but in how far policy engagement has progressed - from early-stage, nonbinding guidelines or strategic documents outlining future directions to fully enforceable laws and regulatory obligations. Capturing this progression is essential for assessing the substantive strength of AI governance, as symbolic or voluntary initiatives signal intent, whereas binding regulation reflects a higher level of institutional consolidation and legal enforceability.

Building on this distinction, we operationalize AI governance strength by assessing the degree to  which  responsible  AI  measures  have  been  institutionally  consolidated.  To  capture  the progression  from  early  policy  engagement  to  legally  binding  regulation,  we  distinguish between four levels of institutionalization. Level 0 indicates the absence of any government action or legislation in a given governance dimension. Level 1 captures non-binding measures, such as voluntary guidelines, ethical frameworks, or strategic policy documents. Level 2 refers to enforceable measures under formal development, including draft legislation or regulatory proposals, which have not been fully enacted yet. Level 3 denotes fully adopted and operational laws or regulations. Together, these levels reflect increasing legal commitment and regulatory strength across governance dimensions.

Examples include Japan's non-mandatory Governance Guidelines addressing impact assessments  (level  1),  Brazil's  drafted Bill  2338/2023 introducing  requirements  for  human oversight and transparency (level 2), and Germany's Federal Data Protection Act or Canada's Directive  on  Automated  Decision-Making ,  both  of  which  establish  enforceable  procedural rights related to redress, accountability, and oversight (level 3).

To  summarize  cross-national  variation  in  the  maturity  of  responsible  AI  governance,  we aggregate these institutionalization levels across the nine governance dimensions into a single AI Governance Strength measure. The index is constructed as the average institutionalization level (coded from 0 to 3) across all dimensions for each country. This approach captures the overall  legal  depth  and  regulatory  consolidation  of  AI  governance,  reflecting  the  extent  to which  governments  have  moved  beyond  aspirational  commitments  toward  binding  and operational  regulatory  frameworks.  For  instance,  a  country  that  relies  exclusively  on  nonbinding guidelines across all dimensions would obtain an index value of 1.0. By contrast, a country with fully enforceable regulation (level 3) in six dimensions and no action (level 0) in the remaining three would obtain an index value of 2.0. By focusing on institutional strength rather  than  the  mere  presence  of  policy  activity,  the  index  provides  a  parsimonious  and substantively meaningful indicator of national AI governance capacity.

Figure  2  maps  the  AI  Governance  Strength  Index  for  countries  included  in  the  full-model sample used in the main analysis.

Figure 2. AI Governance Strength Index across countries included in the main empirical analysis.

## 3.2 National Culture Measures: Hofstede's Cultural Dimensions

To operationalize national culture, we rely on Hofstede's cultural dimensions, one of the most widely used frameworks for cross-national comparison of value systems in the social sciences (Hofstede, 1980, 2001). The framework captures stable national-level orientations related to authority,  individual  autonomy,  risk,  competition,  time  horizons,  and  normative  restraint, which have been shown to shape institutional preferences and governance outcomes across countries.

Country-level  scores  for  all  six  Hofstede  dimensions  -  power  distance,  individualism, masculinity, uncertainty avoidance, long-term orientation, and indulgence - are obtained from the official Hofstede dimension data matrix published by Hofstede Insights (Hofstede, 2015). The dataset synthesizes multiple survey waves and subsequent extensions and is designed to provide comparable national cultural profiles across countries.

All cultural variables are measured on a standardized scale ranging from 0 to 100, with higher values indicating a stronger presence of the respective cultural orientation. Following standard practice in cross-national research, we use Hofstede's country-level scores as stable cultural indicators. These measures ensure consistency and comparability across the countries included in the GIRAI dataset.

## 3.3 Empirical Strategy and Control Variables

We combine our measure of AI Governance Strength with country-level scores on Hofstede's cultural dimensions to examine how national culture relates to the institutional maturity of AI governance.  The  main  analysis  includes  50  countries  for  which  data  are  available  on  AI governance strength and all six Hofstede dimensions.

We estimate a series of ordinary least squares (OLS) regressions with AI Governance Strength as  the  dependent  variable  and  Hofstede's  cultural  dimensions  as  the  main  independent variables. All models are cross-sectional and assess whether systematic variation in national cultural  profiles  is  associated  with  differences  in  the  legal  and  institutional  strength  of  AI governance frameworks.

To account for alternative explanations, we include two control variables capturing countries' economic and institutional capacity. GDP per capita, obtained from the International Monetary Fund (IMF, 2023), is included to control for differences in economic development and resource availability  that  may  affect  governments'  ability  to  design  and  enforce  AI  regulation.  In addition, we control for regulatory quality using the Regulatory Quality indicator from the World Bank's Worldwide Governance Indicators (World Bank, 2024), which captures broader differences  in  policy  design  and  implementation  capacity  across  countries.  Including  these controls  allows  us  to  isolate  the  association  between  national  culture  and  AI  governance strength while holding constant key structural characteristics of the economic and institutional environment.

## 4. Results

## 4.1 National Culture and AI Governance Strength

We first examine how national culture is associated with the overall strength of responsible AI governance. Table 1 reports ordinary least squares regressions with AI Governance Strength as  the  dependent  variable.  All  variables  are  standardized  as  Z-scores,  allowing  direct comparison of coefficient  magnitudes  across  cultural  dimensions.  Columns  (1)-(6)  present models including each cultural dimension separately, while Column (7) reports a full model including all six dimensions simultaneously.

Across specifications, several cultural dimensions exhibit robust and theoretically consistent relationships with AI governance strength. Individualism (H2) shows the strongest and most stable  association.  In  the  full  model,  a  one  standard  deviation  increase  in  individualism  is associated  with  an  increase  of  approximately  0.47  standard  deviations  in  AI  governance strength. This result provides strong support for H2 and suggests that societies emphasizing individual autonomy, rights, and procedural protections tend to adopt more institutionalized and enforceable AI governance frameworks.

Uncertainty  avoidance  (H4)  is  also  positively  and  statistically  significantly  related  to  AI governance strength. In standardized terms, a one standard deviation increase in uncertainty avoidance  corresponds  to  an  increase  of  roughly  0.22  standard  deviations  in  governance strength  in  the  full  model.  This  finding  supports  H4  and  aligns  with  the  expectation  that societies less tolerant of ambiguity respond to AI-related uncertainty through formalized, rulebased regulatory arrangements.

Long-term  orientation  (H5)  exhibits  a  positive  association  with  AI  governance  strength  in models estimated separately (β = 0.195), consistent with the expectation that future-oriented societies invest in durable regulatory institutions. However, this relationship weakens and loses statistical significance in the full model that includes all cultural dimensions simultaneously. Taken together, the results provide partial support for H5, indicating that long-term orientation may be conducive to stronger AI governance, but that its independent contribution is less robust than that of individualism or uncertainty avoidance.

By contrast, indulgence (H6) displays a negative and statistically significant association with AI governance strength. In the full model, a one standard deviation increase in indulgence is associated with a decrease of approximately 0.30 standard deviations in governance strength. This  finding  supports  H6  and  suggests  that  societies  characterized  by  permissiveness  and weaker  normative  restraint  are  less  likely  to  institutionalize  binding  and  enforceable  AI governance frameworks.

The remaining cultural dimensions - power distance (H1) and masculinity (H3) - do not exhibit robust  or  statistically  significant  associations  with  AI  governance  strength.  While  the coefficients  for  masculinity  are  negative,  as  hypothesized,  they  not  reach  conventional significance levels, in both columns (3) and (7). Power distance similarly shows no systematic relationship with AI governance strength. Accordingly, H1 and H3 are not supported by the empirical results.

Overall, our results indicate that national culture explains a substantial share of cross-national variation  in  AI  governance  strength.  Consistent  with  the  theoretical  framework,  the  results highlight  individualism,  uncertainty  avoidance,  and  indulgence  as  the  most  consequential cultural dimensions shaping the institutional maturity of responsible AI governance, even when controlling for economic development and general regulatory capacity.

## 4.2 National Culture and Different Forms of AI Governance

The AI Governance Strength index aggregates variation across different levels of institutionalization, ranging from non-binding initiatives to fully enforceable regulation. To assess  whether  the  observed  cultural  effects  are  driven  by  specific  forms  of  governance initiatives, we disaggregate the index into its underlying institutional components. Specifically, we construct three outcome measures capturing the share of responsible AI governance areas in  which  a  country  has  adopted  (i)  non-binding  measures  such  as  strategies  or  voluntary guidelines  (level  1),  (ii)  draft  or  preparatory  measures  aimed  at  establishing  enforceable regulation (level 2), and (iii) fully enforceable laws or regulations that are legally operational (level 3). Again, Table 2 reports standardized regression results for each outcome.

A clear and consistent pattern emerges across levels of institutionalization. Cultural dimensions are not systematically associated with non-binding or preparatory AI governance measures. In the  specification  where  the  dependent  variable  captures  non-binding  initiatives  such  as voluntary guidelines and strategy documents (Column 2), none of the Hofstede dimensions exhibits a statistically significant association. Similarly, when focusing on draft or preparatory measures  aimed  at  establishing  enforceable  regulation  (Column  3),  cultural  effects  remain largely absent, with only uncertainty avoidance displaying a marginally significant association, and in the opposite direction than expected. Taken together, these results indicate that earlystage engagement with AI governance - whether symbolic or preparatory - show little to no association with national cultural value systems.

By contrast, culture plays a decisive role in explaining the adoption of fully enforceable AI governance frameworks. In the model using enforceable regulation (level 3) as the dependent variable, several cultural dimensions  exhibit statistically significant and substantively meaningful associations. Individualism (H2) is positively related to enforceable AI regulation: a  one  standard  deviation  increase  in  individualism  is  associated  with  an  increase  of approximately  0.54  standard  deviations  in  the  share  of  AI  governance  areas  governed  by binding law. Uncertainty avoidance (H4) likewise shows a positive and significant association (β ≈ 0.53), indicating that societies less tolerant of ambiguity are more likely to institutionalize AI governance through enforceable legal mechanisms.

Conversely, indulgence (H6) is negatively associated with enforceable AI governance. A one standard deviation increase in indulgence corresponds to a decrease of roughly 0.45 standard deviations in enforceable and enacted regulation, supporting the expectation that permissive and  weakly  restrained  societies  are  less  inclined  to  impose  binding  constraints  on  AI deployment.

Controls include: GDP per capita and regulatory quality.

The remaining cultural dimensions - power distance (H1), masculinity (H3), and long-term orientation (H5) - do not exhibit statistically significant associations with fully enforceable AI governance once other cultural traits and controls are taken into account. Taken together, these findings indicate that the cultural drivers of AI governance operate primarily at the level of binding legal institutionalization, rather than at earlier stages of policy engagement. Consistent with the hypothesis framework, we find support for H2, H4, and H6, while H1, H3, and H5 receive no support in this specification.

## 5. Discussion

## 5.1 Summary of Key Findings

This study examined whether and how national cultural value systems shape the strength of responsible AI governance across countries. Using cross-national data from the Global Index on  Responsible  AI  and  Hofstede's  cultural  dimensions,  the  analysis  yields  three  central findings that speak directly to the proposed hypotheses.

First, national culture is systematically related to the overall strength of AI governance, but only  for  a  subset  of  cultural  dimensions.  Strong  and  consistent  support  is  found  for  H2 (Individualism) and H4 (Uncertainty Avoidance), both of which are positively associated with AI governance strength, while H6 (Indulgence) is supported through a negative association. These  results  align  with  the  expectation  that  societies  emphasizing  individual  rights,  risk mitigation, and normative restraint are more likely to institutionalize binding and enforceable AI governance frameworks. By contrast, H1 (Power Distance) and H3 (Masculinity) receive no empirical support, and H5 (Long-Term Orientation) is only partially supported.

Second, disaggregating AI governance by institutional form reveals that cultural effects are not evenly distributed across governance stages. None of the hypotheses receives support when AI governance is operationalized as non-binding initiatives or draft regulatory measures. Instead, the  influence  of  culture  emerges  almost  exclusively  at  the  level  of  fully  enforceable  AI regulation,  indicating  that  hypotheses  related  to  cultural  values  are  most  relevant  when governance  entails  binding  legal  commitment  rather  than  symbolic  or  preparatory  policy engagement.

Overall,  the  findings  demonstrate  that  national  culture  helps  explain  why  some  countries progress from soft-law approaches to enforceable AI governance while others remain at the level  of  voluntary  or  preparatory  measures.  By  explicitly  linking  hypothesis  support  to institutional  form,  the  results  clarify  when  -  and  in  what  ways  -  culture  matters  for  the development of responsible AI governance.

## 5.2 Cultural Foundations of Enforceable AI Governance

A  central  insight  of  this  study  is  that  national  culture  shapes  AI  governance  only  once regulation  becomes  legally  binding.  While  countries  differ  substantially  in  the  adoption  of enforceable AI laws, cultural values play little role in explaining the prevalence of non-binding guidelines or draft regulatory initiatives. This distinction points to a broader governance logic: soft-law instruments are comparatively easy to adopt, often diffuse through international policy networks, and entail limited domestic political or economic cost. As a result, their adoption is less dependent on culturally grounded institutional preferences.

By contrast, enforceable AI regulation imposes concrete legal obligations, compliance costs, and  constraints  on  both  public  authorities  and  private  actors.  Moving  from  aspirational principles  to  binding  law,  therefore,  requires  domestic  legitimacy  and  acceptance  of  legal constraint. The results suggest that this legitimacy is shaped by cultural orientations toward individual  rights,  risk  management,  and  normative  restraint.  Societies  that  emphasize individual autonomy and legal protection, exhibit lower tolerance for uncertainty, and accept behavioral  restraint  are  more  likely  to  view  binding  AI  regulation  as  both  necessary  and appropriate.

This perspective helps explain why global convergence on responsible AI principles has not translated into uniform adoption of enforceable AI governance frameworks. While high-level norms  and  strategies  may  spread  through  emulation  and  international  coordination,  the institutionalization  of  AI  governance  through  law  reflects  deeper,  culturally  embedded expectations about the role of regulation in managing technological risk. Culture, in this sense, does not determine whether countries talk about responsible AI, but whether they are willing to legally commit to it.

## 5.3 Implications for AI Governance and Policy

The findings of this study have important implications for the design, sequencing, and diffusion of AI governance frameworks. Rather than treating AI regulation as a purely technical or legal exercise, the results highlight the role of national cultural context in shaping how - and how far - countries institutionalize responsible AI commitments. In particular, they suggest that differences in regulatory outcomes reflect not only policy capacity or political will, but deeper societal orientations toward risk, authority, and constraint. The implications below outline how these cultural foundations matter for regulatory design, policy implementation, and international coordination.

First, the results caution against one-size-fits-all approaches to AI regulation. Cultural values associated  with  individual  rights  protection,  risk  aversion,  and  normative  restraint  are systematically linked to the adoption of enforceable AI governance. In societies where these orientations are weaker, attempts to rapidly impose binding AI regulation may face political resistance or remain ineffective in practice. This suggests that international efforts to promote AI governance should allow for differentiated pathways, recognizing that institutionalization may proceed at different speeds across cultural contexts. This matters because related research in  human-computer  interaction  shows  that  ethical  alignment,  moderation  practices,  and transparency mechanisms shape user satisfaction and trust in AI products (Lee & Cha, 2025; Pasch & Ha, 2025; Pasch, 2025), implying that differences in regulatory institutionalization may have downstream consequences for how individuals experience AI systems in everyday use.

Second, the findings help explain why many countries remain at the level of strategies, ethical guidelines, or draft legislation, despite widespread acknowledgment of AI-related risks. Softlaw instruments may serve as important signaling devices or learning mechanisms, particularly in culturally heterogeneous environments. However, policymakers should not assume that the existence of such measures necessarily indicates readiness for enforceable regulation. Cultural alignment appears to be a key condition for moving from aspirational commitments to legally binding governance frameworks.

Third, the results have implications for international regulatory diffusion, including initiatives such as the EU AI Act. While comprehensive regulatory models may influence global debates and inspire emulation, their enforceability and effectiveness abroad will depend on compatibility with domestic cultural norms. Policymakers seeking to export or harmonize AI regulation should therefore complement legal templates with capacity-building, stakeholder engagement, and adaptation to local regulatory cultures.

More broadly, the findings underscore that effective AI governance is not solely a technical or legal challenge, but also a socio-cultural one. Designing AI regulation that is both robust and implementable requires attention to the cultural foundations that shape how societies perceive risk,  authority,  and  acceptable  constraint.  Recognizing  these  foundations  may  improve  the prospects for developing AI governance frameworks that are not only formally adopted, but also politically legitimate and institutionally durable.

## 5.4 Limitations and Future Research

While  this  study  provides  novel  empirical  evidence  on  the  cultural  foundations  of  AI governance, several limitations warrant consideration. First, the analysis is cross-sectional and captures associations between national culture and AI governance rather than causal effects. While cultural value systems are commonly understood as slow-moving structural characteristics, future research could adopt longitudinal designs to examine how changes in AI governance  unfold  over  time  and  whether  cultural  factors  condition  the  sequencing  and durability of regulatory reforms.

Second,  this  study  relies  on  Hofstede's  cultural  dimensions  as  country-level  measures  of societal values. Although widely used in cross-national research, this framework represents only one approach to operationalizing national culture and necessarily abstracts from withincountry heterogeneity. As a result, it cannot capture subnational variation or differences across social groups within countries, nor does it account for alternative conceptualizations of culture that  emphasize  values,  norms,  or  beliefs  through  different  lenses.  Future  research  could complement  this  approach  by  drawing  on  alternative  cultural  frameworks,  survey-based measures  of  public  attitudes  toward  AI,  or  regional-level  analyses  to  further  unpack  how cultural values interact with AI governance.

Third,  the  measurement  of  AI  governance  focuses  on  formal  government  action  and  legal institutionalization as captured by the GIRAI dataset. While this allows for systematic crossnational  comparison,  it  does  not  directly  assess  the  quality  of  enforcement,  administrative capacity, or practical effectiveness of AI regulation. Subsequent research could extend this work by examining implementation outcomes, sector-specific governance arrangements, or firm-level compliance with AI-related rules.

Finally, while this study focuses on cultural value systems and controls for broad economic and regulatory conditions, future research could extend the analysis by explicitly incorporating political dynamics such as regime type, partisan competition, or interest group mobilization. Examining how these political factors interact with national cultural values may yield deeper insights  into  the  processes  through  which  AI  governance  frameworks  are  negotiated, institutionalized, and contested.

## 6. Conclusion

This study provides systematic cross-national evidence that national culture shapes the strength of responsible AI governance. Drawing on data from the Global Index on Responsible AI and Hofstede's  cultural  dimensions,  the  analysis  shows  that  individualism  and  uncertainty avoidance  are  positively  associated  with  stronger  AI  governance,  while  indulgence  is negatively related, whereas power distance, masculinity, and long-term orientation exhibit no robust independent effects.

Importantly, these cultural associations emerge only at the level of enforceable AI regulation. Cultural values are not systematically related to the presence of voluntary guidelines or draft regulatory  initiatives,  suggesting  that  early-stage  AI  governance  is  largely  shaped  by international  diffusion  and  policy  signaling.  By  contrast,  the  transition  to  binding  and operational AI law appears to depend on culturally grounded expectations about individual rights, risk management, and acceptable regulatory constraint.

Taken  together,  the  findings  highlight  that  AI  governance  is  not  only  a  technical  or  legal challenge, but a culturally embedded institutional process. While global convergence around responsible AI principles is widespread, the adoption of enforceable regulation reflects deeper societal values that shape how  countries translate shared norms into binding legal commitments.  Recognizing  these  cultural  foundations  can  help  explain  persistent  crossnational variation in AI governance and inform more context-sensitive approaches to global AI regulation.
